reasoning:
The ICE BofA US High Yield B-rated OAS spiked dramatically from 3.53 on April 2 to a peak of 4.86 on April 7 due to an unexpected, massive tariff escalation between the US (145%) and China (125%). However, over the past four trading days (April 8-11), the spread has steadily mean-reverted downward, closing at 4.46 on Friday, April 11, despite the official announcement of China's retaliatory tariffs on that very day. This signals that the immediate market panic is subsiding and the spread is likely to continue its short-term retracement back toward the 3.8-4.2 range. Therefore, the probability of the spread being higher than 4.46 on the near-term dates (April 20 and May 13) is relatively low. 

Looking further ahead to July and October, the structural economic damage from such extreme tariffs will begin to impact corporate Q2/Q3 earnings, substantially increasing the risk of high-yield defaults or a broader macroeconomic slowdown. This fundamentally increases the probability that spreads could blow out again (potentially exceeding 5.0). Reflecting both this rising fundamental risk and general long-horizon uncertainty, the probability of the spread being higher than the April 13 baseline climbs as the year progresses, landing near 50% by the fall.
